reasoning: The Lean statement parameterizes by T > 0 (time difference between start times), treats car 1 as starting at time 0 with laps = ⌊t⌋, and car 2 as starting at time T with laps = ⌊t-T⌋. The set {t | t ≥ T ∧ ⌊t⌋ = 2⌊t-T⌋} correctly captures "times when both cars are running and car 1 has exactly twice as many laps as car 2," and Lebesgue measure matches "total amount of time." A direct computation confirms the measure equals 1 for every T > 0. The t ≥ T restriction surfaces an implicit premise needed for the claim to be nontrivially true (without it, Nat.floor being 0 on negatives would make the set have infinite measure). The use of Nat.floor on nonneg reals agrees with the usual floor. The units ("1 lap/hour"/"1 hour") and the "exactly twice" equality are all correctly rendered. This is a faithful formalization.
